summary: In this interview, Mark Zuckerberg discusses Meta's aggressive investments in AI and AR/VR, emphasizing how AI will transform advertising into an automated 'business results machine' and make smart glasses the ultimate AI form factor. He also shares his unconventional management philosophy, which includes minimizing recurring meetings, rejecting traditional delegation, and relying on a densely connected 'small group' of leaders rather than strict hierarchy.

principles.statement: I basically don't believe in delegation.
principles.rationale: If you are running a company and are ultimately on the hook for everything, you should be directly involved in any decision you deem important, regardless of the level of detail.
principles.source_quote: I basically don't believe in delegation... if you're running the company and you're on the hook for everything and there's like something that's important at whatever level of detail in the organization, I don't get like the logic of saying like I don't I'm not going to be involved in that.

principles.statement: Keep your schedule open to allow for dynamic focus.
principles.rationale: Having a fully scheduled day prevents you from working on the most important things that arise organically, which leads to frustration and inefficiency.
principles.source_quote: I get really frustrated and bad mood if like my whole day is scheduled and there's like a thing that I know is really important and I like don't get time to do it because I'm sitting in other things that are not the most important thing to be doing.

principles.statement: We must play a role in shaping the next computing platform.
principles.rationale: Delivering services through competitors (like Apple and Google) is complicated, limits the consumer features you can build, and subjects the business to arbitrary taxes and restrictions.
principles.source_quote: It is awkward, right, to um to basically um have the main kind of way that you distribute your service... it's complicated delivering your your services through competitors who are trying to compete with your thing.

principles.statement: Getting things done is a people-driven thing, not an org chart thing.
principles.rationale: A dense network of long-tenured leaders who trust each other and understand each other's strengths and weaknesses is far more effective than a rigid hierarchy.
principles.source_quote: you want to have a dense network of connections so that way you know it's ultimately like getting things done is a people driven thing um as much as it is kind of an intellectual exercise.

frameworks.name: Small Group Context Sharing
frameworks.when_to_apply: When running a large organization and needing to maintain agility without relying on strict hierarchical reporting.
steps: Identify a core group of 25-30 key leaders across the company, regardless of direct reporting lines.
Spend significant time together as a group going through all the context of everything happening across the company.
Use this highly connected, high-trust group as a 'core army' to execute cross-functional priorities.
frameworks.source_quote: I have this group that I sort of ironically call small group, but it's like you know 25 to 30 people... I invest a lot of time in like making sure that there's a group that's very connected to each other and that have all the context on what's going on inside the company and that's sort of like the core army that can go get like anything done.

mental_models.name: AI as a Business Results Machine
mental_models.description: Viewing advertising not as buying placements or targeting demographics, but as an automated system where a business inputs a goal and a budget, and the AI handles the creative, targeting, and delivery to maximize results.
mental_models.example: A business connects their bank account, states they want to acquire new customers for a specific price, and the AI generates 4,000 versions of creative, tests them, and delivers the results without human intervention.

mental_models.name: Glasses as the Ultimate AI Form Factor
mental_models.description: Viewing smart glasses not just as a display device, but as the perfect continuous sensory input for an AI assistant.
mental_models.example: Instead of starting a query from scratch on a phone, the AI in your glasses sees what you see and hears what you hear throughout the day, building continuous personal context.

heuristics: If you're going to report to me, you need to be able to manage yourself.
Being even more ambitious has been more predictive over the last few years about where things are likely going to be in the industry.
Think about long-term bets in terms of people rather than finances: allocate the best people to the most important things and let them eat first.

signature_quotes.text: I basically don't believe in delegation.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing his management philosophy and involvement in company decisions.

signature_quotes.text: Glasses are kind of the perfect form factor for AI because you can let an AI see what you see and hear what you hear and talk to you throughout the day.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining Meta's consumer AI strategy and hardware investments.

signature_quotes.text: It's complicated delivering your services through competitors who are trying to compete with your thing.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the strategic rationale for investing heavily in AR/VR to bypass Apple and Google.

signature_quotes.text: I get really frustrated and bad mood if like my whole day is scheduled and there's like a thing that I know is really important and I like don't get time to do it.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing why he minimizes recurring meetings to keep his schedule open.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Advertisers often think they know their exact demographic, but the AI is actually better at finding interested customers if left unconstrained.

anti_patterns.description: Viewing AI primarily as a tool to replace human jobs.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It's the wrong mental model. AI is better viewed as a tool that provides 10x leverage, making previously cost-prohibitive services (like voice customer support for billions of users) economically viable.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on strict org charts and hierarchy to get things done.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Getting things done is a people-driven process. Without a dense network of trust and shared context among leaders, execution suffers.

anti_patterns.description: Filling a CEO's schedule with recurring standing meetings.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It prevents the leader from dynamically focusing on the most important tasks that arise day-to-day, leading to frustration.
